
## Text formatting {#sec-text}

Standard Markdown formatting: *italic*, **bold**, ***bold italic***, ~~strikethrough~~, `inline code`.

Superscript and subscript: H~2~O is water. 2^10^ is 1024.

[Small caps text]{.smallcaps} and [underlined text]{.underline} and [highlighted text]{.mark}.

A keyboard shortcut: {{< kbd Ctrl-Shift-P >}}

---

## Callout blocks {#sec-callouts}

Quarto provides five callout types with three appearance styles.

### Default appearance

::: {.callout-note}
## Note
Use notes for supplementary information that adds context.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## Tip
Use tips for best practices and helpful suggestions.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## Warning
Use warnings for actions that could cause problems if ignored.
:::

::: {.callout-caution}
## Caution
Use caution for irreversible actions or potential data loss.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## Important
Use important for critical information that must not be missed.
:::

### Collapsible callout

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Click to expand
This content is hidden by default. Useful for long examples, solutions, or supplementary details that would otherwise interrupt the reading flow.
:::

### Simple and minimal appearance

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
## Simple appearance
A lighter callout style without the colored background header.
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
## Minimal appearance
The most lightweight callout — just a colored left border.
:::

### No icon

::: {.callout-tip icon=false}
## Without icon
Callouts can suppress their icon for a cleaner look.
:::

---

## Code blocks {#sec-code}

### Syntax highlighting

```python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def gaussian(x, mu=0, sigma=1):
    """Compute the Gaussian probability density function."""
    return (1 / (sigma * np.sqrt(2 * np.pi))) * np.exp(-0.5 * ((x - mu) / sigma) ** 2)

x = np.linspace(-4, 4, 200)
plt.plot(x, gaussian(x), label="μ=0, σ=1")
plt.plot(x, gaussian(x, mu=1, sigma=0.5), label="μ=1, σ=0.5")
plt.legend()
plt.title("Gaussian Distributions")
plt.show()
```

### Code with filename

```{.python filename="analysis.py"}
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv("data/experiment_001.csv")
summary = df.describe()
print(summary)
```

### Line numbers and highlighting

```{.python code-line-numbers="3-5"}
import numpy as np

data = np.loadtxt("measurements.csv", delimiter=",")
mean_val = np.mean(data, axis=0)
std_val = np.std(data, axis=0)

print(f"Mean: {mean_val}, Std: {std_val}")
```

Lines 3–5 are highlighted above. Line numbers appear on all lines.

### Code annotations

```python
import pandas as pd                        # <1>

df = pd.read_csv("data.csv")              # <2>
df = df.dropna(subset=["measurement"])     # <3>
result = df.groupby("condition").mean()    # <4>
```
1. Import the pandas library for data manipulation.
2. Load experimental data from a CSV file.
3. Remove rows with missing measurement values.
4. Compute mean per experimental condition.

---

## Cross-references {#sec-crossrefs}

Quarto can cross-reference figures, tables, equations, sections, code listings, and theorems.

### Section references

See @sec-callouts for callout examples, @sec-code for code blocks, or @sec-equations for math.

### Figure references

See @fig-placeholder below.

![A placeholder figure demonstrating cross-referencing.](/images/placeholder-wide.svg){#fig-placeholder}

### Table references

See @tbl-elements for a cross-referenced table.

| Element | Symbol | Atomic number |
|---------|:------:|:-------------:|
| Hydrogen  | H  | 1 |
| Carbon    | C  | 6 |
| Nitrogen  | N  | 7 |
| Oxygen    | O  | 8 |
| Phosphorus| P  | 15 |

: Selected elements relevant to biology. {#tbl-elements}

### Equation references

The Nernst equation (@eq-nernst) describes the equilibrium potential of an ion:

$$
E = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{outside}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{inside}}}
$$ {#eq-nernst}

### Code listing references

See @lst-query for an example SQL query.

```{#lst-query .sql lst-cap="Example database query for experimental sessions."}
SELECT session_id, subject, date, trial_count
FROM experiments
WHERE condition = 'control'
ORDER BY date DESC;
```

---

## Figures {#sec-figures}

### Basic figure with sizing

![A smaller placeholder image (300px wide).](/images/placeholder-small.svg){width=300 fig-align="center"}

### Figure alignment

::: {layout="[1,1,1]"}
![Left](/images/placeholder-left.svg){fig-align="left"}

![Center](/images/placeholder-center.svg){fig-align="center"}

![Right](/images/placeholder-right.svg){fig-align="right"}
:::

### Subfigures with layout

::: {#fig-panels layout-ncol=2}
![Panel A: Control condition](/images/placeholder-a.svg){#fig-panel-a}

![Panel B: Experimental condition](/images/placeholder-b.svg){#fig-panel-b}

Comparison of control and experimental conditions. See @fig-panel-a for the control.
:::

### Custom layout with spacing

::: {layout="[[1,1], [1]]"}
![Top left](/images/placeholder-1.svg)

![Top right](/images/placeholder-2.svg)

![Full width bottom](/images/placeholder-3.svg)
:::

### Lightbox

Click the image below to view it enlarged (HTML only).

![Click to enlarge this image.](/images/placeholder-large.svg){.lightbox fig-align="center" width=500}

---

## Tables {#sec-tables}

### Pipe table with alignment

| Left-aligned | Centered | Right-aligned | Default |
|:-------------|:--------:|--------------:|---------|
| Row 1        | Data     | 3.14          | Alpha   |
| Row 2        | Data     | 2.72          | Beta    |
| Row 3        | Data     | 1.62          | Gamma   |

: Demonstration of column alignment. {#tbl-alignment}

### Grid table

Grid tables support block-level content in cells (lists, paragraphs, code).

+---------------+-------------------+------------------+
| Method        | Advantages        | Disadvantages    |
+===============+===================+==================+
| Two-photon    | - Deep imaging    | - Expensive      |
|               | - Less phototoxic | - Slow scan rate |
+---------------+-------------------+------------------+
| Confocal      | - Good resolution | - Phototoxic     |
|               | - Common          | - Limited depth  |
+---------------+-------------------+------------------+
| Widefield     | - Fast            | - Low contrast   |
|               | - Simple setup    | - No sectioning  |
+---------------+-------------------+------------------+

: Comparison of imaging modalities. {#tbl-imaging}

### Bootstrap-styled table (HTML)

| Strain | Genotype | Phenotype | Available |
|--------|----------|-----------|-----------|
| Line A | +/+      | Wild-type | Yes       |
| Line B | mut/+    | Het       | Yes       |
| Line C | mut/mut  | Knockout  | No        |

: Mouse lines with striped rows and hover effect. {.striped .hover #tbl-strains}

### Column widths

| Col A | Col B | Col C |
|-------|-------|-------|
| Wide  | Med   | Narrow|

: Custom column widths. {tbl-colwidths="[50,30,20]" #tbl-widths}

---

## Tabsets {#sec-tabsets}

### Analysis in multiple languages

::: {.panel-tabset}

## Python
```python
import numpy as np

data = np.random.normal(0, 1, 1000)
mean = np.mean(data)
std = np.std(data)
print(f"Mean: {mean:.3f}, Std: {std:.3f}")
```

## R
```r
data <- rnorm(1000, mean = 0, sd = 1)
cat(sprintf("Mean: %.3f, Std: %.3f\n", mean(data), sd(data)))
```

## MATLAB
```matlab
data = randn(1000, 1);
fprintf('Mean: %.3f, Std: %.3f\n', mean(data), std(data));
```

:::

### Protocol steps

::: {.panel-tabset}

## Day 1: Preparation
- Prepare solutions A and B
- Calibrate equipment
- Run control checks

## Day 2: Recording
- Anesthetize animal
- Perform surgery
- Begin recording session

## Day 3: Analysis
- Export data files
- Run preprocessing pipeline
- Perform statistical tests

:::

---

## Equations {#sec-equations}

### Inline math

The membrane potential $V_m$ is approximately $-70$ mV at rest. The time constant is $\tau = RC$.

### Display equations

The Hodgkin-Huxley model describes the action potential:

$$
C_m \frac{dV}{dt} = -g_{Na} m^3 h (V - E_{Na}) - g_K n^4 (V - E_K) - g_L (V - E_L) + I_{ext}
$$ {#eq-hh}

The diffusion equation in one dimension:

$$
\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}
$$ {#eq-diffusion}

### Multiple referenced equations

The signal-to-noise ratio (@eq-snr) and the Shannon entropy (@eq-entropy):

$$
\text{SNR} = \frac{\mu_{\text{signal}}}{\sigma_{\text{noise}}}
$$ {#eq-snr}

$$
H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)
$$ {#eq-entropy}

---

## Diagrams {#sec-diagrams}

### Mermaid flowchart

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-pipeline
%%| fig-cap: "Typical data analysis pipeline."
flowchart LR
  A[Raw Data] --> B[Preprocessing]
  B --> C{Quality Check}
  C -->|Pass| D[Analysis]
  C -->|Fail| E[Review & Fix]
  E --> B
  D --> F[Visualization]
  D --> G[Statistics]
  F --> H[Publication]
  G --> H
```

### Mermaid sequence diagram

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-protocol
%%| fig-cap: "Experimental protocol communication flow."
sequenceDiagram
  participant E as Experimenter
  participant S as Software
  participant H as Hardware
  E->>S: Start session
  S->>H: Initialize DAQ
  H-->>S: Ready
  S-->>E: Recording started
  loop Each trial
    E->>S: Trigger stimulus
    S->>H: Send TTL pulse
    H-->>S: Data stream
  end
  E->>S: End session
  S->>H: Stop acquisition
  S-->>E: Data saved
```

### Mermaid gantt chart

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-timeline
%%| fig-cap: "Project timeline."
gantt
  title Project Alpha Timeline
  dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD
  section Design
    Literature review     :done,    d1, 2025-09-01, 30d
    Prototype design      :done,    d2, after d1, 45d
  section Build
    Component sourcing    :active,  b1, 2026-01-15, 30d
    Assembly              :         b2, after b1, 60d
  section Testing
    Validation            :         t1, after b2, 30d
    Documentation         :         t2, after t1, 20d
```

### Graphviz directed graph

```{dot}
//| label: fig-architecture
//| fig-cap: "System architecture."
digraph G {
  rankdir=TB;
  node [shape=box, style="rounded,filled", fillcolor="#e8f4f8", fontname="Helvetica"];
  edge [color="#2c5f7c"];

  Sensors [fillcolor="#2c5f7c", fontcolor=white];
  DAQ [label="Data Acquisition"];
  Processing [label="Real-time\nProcessing"];
  Storage [label="Data Storage"];
  Analysis [label="Offline\nAnalysis"];
  Viz [label="Visualization"];

  Sensors -> DAQ -> Processing;
  Processing -> Storage;
  Storage -> Analysis;
  Analysis -> Viz;
  Processing -> Viz [style=dashed, label="live"];
}
```

---

## Citations and footnotes {#sec-citations}

### Citations

Parenthetical citation: [@einstein1905]. Narrative citation: @einstein1905 demonstrated the theory of special relativity. With locator: [see @einstein1905, p. 891]. Suppress author: [-@einstein1905].

### Footnotes

Standard footnote reference[^1]. Another footnote[^2].

[^1]: This is a standard footnote. It appears at the bottom of the page in HTML (hover to preview) and at the bottom in PDF.

[^2]: Footnotes can contain **formatting**, `code`, and even [links](https://example.com).

Inline footnotes are also supported^[Like this one, defined right in the text.].

---

## Layout {#sec-layout}

### Margin content

This paragraph has an aside in the margin. [Margin notes are useful for supplementary information, definitions, or commentary that supports but doesn't interrupt the main text.]{.aside}

### Column widths

:::{.column-body-outset}
This content extends slightly beyond the body text width, useful for wider figures or tables that need a bit more room.

| A | B | C | D | E | F | G | H |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |

:::

:::{.column-page}
This content extends to the full page width. Use this for wide figures, large tables, or panoramic images.
:::

### Definition list

Electrophysiology
:   The study of electrical properties of biological cells and tissues. Includes techniques such as patch-clamp recording and extracellular recording.

Optogenetics
:   A technique that uses light to control neurons that have been genetically modified to express light-sensitive ion channels.

Calcium imaging
:   A technique for measuring intracellular calcium concentration using fluorescent indicators, commonly used to infer neural activity.

---

## Lists {#sec-lists}

### Task list

- [x] Design experimental protocol
- [x] Order components
- [ ] Assemble hardware
- [ ] Validate system
- [ ] Write documentation

### Nested ordered list

1. Preprocessing pipeline
   i) Motion correction
      a. Rigid body alignment
      b. Non-rigid registration
   ii) Denoising
   iii) Normalization
2. Feature extraction
3. Statistical analysis
   i) Univariate tests
   ii) Multivariate methods

---

## Conditional content {#sec-conditional}

The following content changes depending on the output format:

::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}
**You are viewing the HTML version.** This format supports interactive features like hover citations, tabsets, search, and lightbox images.
:::

::: {.content-visible when-format="typst"}
**You are viewing the PDF version.** This format is optimized for printing with a two-column layout and numbered sections.
:::

::: {.content-visible when-format="docx"}
**You are viewing the Word version.** Use Track Changes to suggest edits.
:::

::: {.content-visible when-format="gfm"}
**You are viewing the Markdown version.** This plain-text format is useful for LLM processing and version control diffs.
:::

---

## Theorem environments {#sec-theorems}

::: {#thm-bayes}
## Bayes' Theorem
For events $A$ and $B$ where $P(B) > 0$:

$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \, P(A)}{P(B)}
$$
:::

::: {#def-entropy}
## Shannon Entropy
The **Shannon entropy** of a discrete random variable $X$ with possible values $\{x_1, \ldots, x_n\}$ is:

$$
H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i) \log_2 P(x_i)
$$
:::

::: {#lem-triangle}
## Triangle Inequality
For any vectors $\mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{v}$: $\|\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v}\| \leq \|\mathbf{u}\| + \|\mathbf{v}\|$.
:::

::: {.proof}
## Proof of @lem-triangle
By the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality...  *(proof omitted for brevity)*
:::

::: {#exm-poisson}
## Poisson Distribution
If a neuron fires at an average rate of $\lambda = 5$ spikes/s, the probability of observing $k$ spikes in a 1-second window is:

$$
P(X = k) = \frac{\lambda^k e^{-\lambda}}{k!}
$$
:::

By @thm-bayes, we can update our beliefs given new data. The entropy defined in @def-entropy quantifies uncertainty. See @exm-poisson for a practical application.

---

## Shortcodes {#sec-shortcodes}

### Document metadata

This document is titled "{{< meta title >}}" and was last updated on {{< meta date >}}.

### Keyboard shortcuts

Copy: {{< kbd Ctrl-C >}} | Paste: {{< kbd Ctrl-V >}} | Save: {{< kbd Ctrl-S >}}

Platform-specific: {{< kbd mac=Cmd-S win=Ctrl-S linux=Ctrl-S >}}

### Page break

The following inserts a page break (visible in PDF/DOCX output):

{{< pagebreak >}}

### Placeholder text

{{< lipsum 1 >}}

---

## Embedded video {#sec-video}

::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}
{{< video https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_ue8EXv8HIM title="Example embedded video" >}}
:::

::: {.content-visible unless-format="html"}
*Video embeds are only available in the HTML version of this document.*
:::

---

## Version

Document version: {{< meta git-version >}}

Rendered with Quarto {{< version >}}.

## References
